title: Open Workshop: Mistake Recovery, Cost Plus Pricing, and Managing Client Time
summary: Participants brought live business challenges to Erin and the group for real-time problem solving. Topics included how to handle a measurement mistake with a client, how to structure cost plus pricing and management fees for high-end acreage builds, and how to manage time-consuming client meetings when scaling a custom home building company.

teachings.concept: Closing the Loop on Mistakes Without Reopening the Wound
teachings.explanation: When a builder makes a mistake, addresses it honestly, presents solutions, and absorbs the cost, the matter should be considered closed. Offering additional compensation or continuing to apologize after resolution risks highlighting the error further and undermining confidence in the relationship.
teachings.quote: I would never drag anything else into it because that like, to me, when we start, like, do you just feel bad or you're worried about the relationship?

teachings.concept: Full Client Buy-In on Consequence Decisions
teachings.explanation: When a mistake leads to a delay or alternative path, the client must fully own whatever decision is made. If they choose the faster workaround over the correct fix, that decision must be documented and understood so it cannot be used as leverage against the builder later.
teachings.quote: I would always make sure that they fully own that decision. And if the decision is, I know it's going to push us back a week, I've done this. I said, if you're renting a house, I'll pay for the month. Because I don't ever want that to be a negotiation for the future.

teachings.concept: Fixed Price vs. Cost Plus Based on Certainty Level
teachings.explanation: Fixed price contracts work best when project details are highly certain, allowing the builder to protect margins. Cost plus is better suited for projects with significant unknowns, such as large acreage sites, because it protects the builder from absorbing unpredictable site costs while being transparent with the client.
teachings.quote: Fixed price is great when you're pretty close to a hundred percent certain. And it seems like how that model works well for you and your profit margins.

teachings.concept: Transparency of the Insurance Premium in Fixed Price
teachings.explanation: In a fixed price model, the builder must include a risk buffer to account for market changes and unknowns. Erin teaches clients that this buffer is essentially an insurance policy for both parties, and making that explicit removes misunderstanding about why fixed price may cost more upfront.
teachings.quote: It makes sense for everybody because in a fixed price model, there's an insurance policy in there. I've even explained that if you want to go fixed price, then there's a huge insurance policy here for all of us. And that's in the cost.

teachings.concept: Cost Plus Fee Structure Including Project Manager Cost
teachings.explanation: In a cost plus arrangement, the builder's fee sits above the cost of work and must cover the project manager's fully loaded cost, general liability, and profit. The project manager cost can either be embedded in the cost of work or listed separately as its own line item so clients understand what they are paying for.
teachings.quote: There's a fee. So cost plus, then the fee is charged to that cost below that is our project manager cost, our general liability cost and our profit.

teachings.concept: Pre-Construction Strategic Plan to Flush Out Unknowns
teachings.explanation: Before committing to a full service scope on a complex project, charging a fixed conceptual or pre-construction fee to document all unknowns, align all parties, and set expectations reduces risk for the builder, architect, designer, and client. This phase produces documentation that guides the entire team before detailed design begins.
teachings.quote: It happens before we get into the detailed design pieces of it, but it helps flesh out and really level expectations across all of the parties, the client, the contractor, the architect, the interior designer.

teachings.concept: Good Better Best Finish Budgeting for Early Scope Control
teachings.explanation: Assigning a good, better, or best finish category to each specification area early in the design process allows the team to set realistic budget ranges before final selections are made. This prevents designers from over-specifying in areas where budget has already been committed to structural or engineering needs.
teachings.quote: We look at it as is it going to be like a good, better or best kind of finish option, which allows us to put kind of a budget number and risk assessment with those items.

teachings.concept: Scaling Out of the Bottleneck with Project Managers and an Executive Assistant
teachings.explanation: When a builder is the sole point of contact for clients, trades, and staff across multiple large projects, every constraint runs through one person. Hiring experienced project managers and an executive assistant who filters and categorizes communications are the two primary levers that free the owner to operate at a higher level.
teachings.quote: You just have a people problem. You need people.

actionable_advice: Once a client mistake is resolved and paid for, do not offer additional compensation or apologize further; tie a knot in it and move on.
If a client chooses a timeline-saving workaround over a full rebuild, ensure they formally own that decision in writing so it cannot be used against you later.
If a client is renting during a delay caused by a builder mistake, offer to cover that rental cost directly to eliminate any future leverage or resentment.
For acreage or high-unknown projects, use cost plus pricing rather than fixed price and communicate your certainty level per line item in the budget openly with the client.
When pricing project manager cost in a cost plus fee, take their market salary, divide it across active projects, then add approximately 30% to cover payroll taxes, insurance, and related costs.

Use a shared pre-construction specification document that lists every finish and system room by room, tracking what is decided versus unknown, and allow designers, architects, and the builder to update it collaboratively.
Set a fixed weekly on-site meeting time with the client, architect, and designer as the official forum for all project decisions and discourage ad hoc issue-raising outside that meeting.
Send clients a pre-meeting agenda and ask them to add their items before the meeting so you can prepare answers in advance and keep the meeting efficient.
Set a hard stop for every client meeting and communicate it at the start; even if your schedule is open, giving clients a defined end time trains them to show up prepared and focused.

Hire project managers with a background in production home building so their core scheduling and trade management skills are already developed and you only need to train them on your company's specific structure.
client_challenges: Bennett made a measurement mistake on cabinetry and was unsure whether to offer a discount or additional free work to protect a newer client relationship after resolving the issue.
Lauren is considering transitioning from fixed price to cost plus for two large acreage custom home builds worth three to five million dollars where site costs are highly uncertain.
Lauren was unsure how to structure and price a management fee within a cost plus model, particularly whether the project manager cost should be inside or outside the cost of work.
Lauren does not yet have a project manager on staff and needs her fee structure to account for both her own time now and a future project manager she plans to hire.

Christine has six to seven large custom home builds at a five million dollar plus price point and is overwhelmed trying to personally manage client meetings, trade questions, staff direction, and email volume.
Christine struggles to delegate because she has high standards and does not yet have time to train new hires, creating a bottleneck she cannot get out of.
Christine finds client meetings running two to three hours and asked how to structure meetings to be more productive without sacrificing client relationship quality.
notable_quotes: On resolving mistakes: 'Tie a big old knot in that one and make sure it stays.'
On client leverage after a mistake: 'That will drip like a little faucet and then it'll be in a, some things expected. Kind of holding something over you.'
On fixed price risk: 'If you want to go fixed price, then there's a huge insurance policy here for all of us. And that's in the cost.'
On transparency with clients: 'My rule of thumb is if I'm afraid to say something, I'm just going to put it out there.'
On Christine's staffing situation: 'You just have a people problem. You need people.'

On building trust with new project managers: 'It takes six months for people to understand what decision would you actually make. They have to learn how you think. And once they're clear on that, then they're good to go.'
